title: Tarka dhal
subtitle: Yellow split lentils with cumin-tempered ghee
excerpt: Yellow split lentils simmered with turmeric until completely soft, seasoned with salt and lemon, then finished with a hot tarka of ghee, cumin seeds, mustard seeds, garlic, and dried chilli poured sizzling over the top. The tarka finishes the dish in one aromatic pour rather than being cooked through — the contrast between the mild, smooth dal and the sharp, fragrant oil defines the dish.
sourceNotes: Tarka dhal (also dal tadka) is a foundational dish of North Indian and Pakistani cooking, popularised in Britain through the curry-house tradition. It is listed in virtually every British Indian restaurant menu and documented across South Asian food writing including Madhur Jaffrey's 'An Invitation to Indian Cooking' (1973).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to 3 months. Thickens on freezing — add water when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a large pot and reheat across the week.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the dal ahead. Make the tarka fresh just before serving.

content.text: The dal cooks in two phases. The first is a long, gentle simmer that turns the lentils completely soft and then breaks them down further with vigorous stirring into a smooth, thick porridge. The second is the tarka — a small pan of ghee heated until shimmering, then seeds, garlic, and chilli added in sequence until the garlic is golden and the seeds are popping. This is poured straight over the finished dal at the table. The sizzle is part of the ritual.

Red lentils cook faster and are common in the British version. Chana dal (split chickpeas) takes longer but gives a slightly firmer texture. Either works well. The turmeric goes into the water with the lentils from the start, which gives the dal its yellow colour and prevents the raw turmeric flavour.

Put the {{red-lentils}} in a saucepan with the {{turmeric}} and 900 ml water. Bring to the boil, skim off any foam, reduce the heat, and simmer uncovered for 25 to 30 minutes until completely soft and beginning to break down. Stir vigorously to help them dissolve into a smooth, thick consistency. Season with {{lemon}} juice and salt. Add water if needed to reach a thick but pourable consistency.

Heat the {{ghee}} in a small pan over high heat until shimmering. Add the {{mustard-seeds-black}} and {{cumin-seeds}}: they will pop immediately. Add the {{garlic}} slices and the {{chilli-red}} if using. Cook for 30 to 60 seconds until the garlic is just golden. Immediately pour the entire contents of the pan over the dal — it should sizzle loudly. Scatter over fresh coriander and serve with rice or naan.

items.symptom: Dal is grainy rather than smooth
items.fix: Continue simmering and stirring — red lentils need a good 25 to 30 minutes. After that, stir vigorously to break them down. Adding a splash of hot water helps

items.cause: Garlic allowed to burn rather than golden
items.fix: Once the garlic goes golden it burns within seconds. Have the dal ready in a serving dish before starting the tarka, and pour immediately when the garlic is just golden
Add hot water gradually until the texture coats a spoon but still moves. It should pool rather than stand
Lentils absorb a lot of seasoning. Taste after the tarka is added and adjust salt and lemon

content.text: Tarka dhal is the vegetarian cornerstone of the British curry-house table, ordered as a side dish alongside meat curries or as a light main with rice and naan. Its combination of lentil simplicity and aromatic finish is the same principle behind countless South Asian dal preparations — the tarka technique (tempering whole spices in hot fat and pouring over the finished dish) is one of the foundational cooking methods of the subcontinent. The British version is faithful to that technique even when simplified.
